overview:
## Overview

After you have an identity, you can extract the private key from it with a call to the [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SecIdentityCopyPrivateKey(_:_:)] function:

Similarly, you can extract the certificate with a call to the [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SecIdentityCopyCertificate(_:_:)] function:

In both cases, you inspect the returned status value to determine whether an error occurred during the extraction. In Objective-C, you are responsible for freeing the associated memory with a call to [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/CoreFoundation/CFRelease] when you’re done with these objects. In Swift, the system manages the memory automatically, releasing it when the object goes out of scope.

# Parsing an Identity

**Article**

Extract the private key and certificate from an identity.

## Overview

After you have an identity, you can extract the private key from it with a call to the [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SecIdentityCopyPrivateKey(_:_:)] function:



Similarly, you can extract the certificate with a call to the [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SecIdentityCopyCertificate(_:_:)] function:



In both cases, you inspect the returned status value to determine whether an error occurred during the extraction. In Objective-C, you are responsible for freeing the associated memory with a call to [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/CoreFoundation/CFRelease] when you’re done with these objects. In Swift, the system manages the memory automatically, releasing it when the object goes out of scope.
title: Parsing an Identity
